expanded_description: A clear introduction to the bryophyte life cycle for secondary school biology. This covers how mosses, liverworts, and hornworts reproduce—alternating between a green, leafy gametophyte (the plant we see) and a dependent sporophyte (the stalk with a capsule) that makes spores.

core_concept.principle: Bryophytes (mosses, liverworts, hornworts) have a life cycle with two generations. The green, leafy plant we see is the gametophyte (haploid, one set of chromosomes). It produces eggs and sperm. Fertilization requires water. The resulting sporophyte (diploid, two sets of chromosomes) grows attached to the gametophyte and makes spores.
core_concept.explanation:
If you look at a moss growing on a rock, what you see is the gametophyte. It is green and leafy, and it does all the photosynthesis. It is the dominant generation. The gametophyte is haploid—each cell has one set of chromosomes.

The gametophyte produces gametes (sex cells). Male gametes (sperm) are made in structures called antheridia. Female gametes (eggs) are made in structures called archegonia. The sperm have flagella (tails) and need to swim through water to reach the eggs. This is why mosses grow in damp places—they need water for fertilization.

When a sperm fertilizes an egg, it forms a zygote. The zygote is diploid—it has two sets of chromosomes. It grows into the sporophyte. The sporophyte is the stalk with a capsule at the top. It is attached to the gametophyte and gets its food from it. It does not photosynthesize much (or at all).

Inside the capsule, special cells called sporocytes undergo meiosis. Meiosis reduces the number of chromosomes by half, producing haploid spores. The spores are released and carried by the wind. When a spore lands in a damp place, it germinates and grows into a new gametophyte. The cycle starts over.

This alternating pattern—gametophyte to sporophyte to gametophyte—is called alternation of generations. In bryophytes, the gametophyte is the dominant, long-lived stage. In ferns and flowering plants, the sporophyte is dominant. Understanding this cycle helps us understand how plants evolved to live on land.

image.alt: A diagram of the moss life cycle showing the green gametophyte, the sporophyte growing from it, and spores being released
image.caption: Moss life cycle: Gametophyte (green, leafy) makes sperm and eggs → water needed for fertilization → zygote grows into sporophyte (stalk + capsule) → sporophyte makes spores via meiosis → spores grow into new gametophytes. Gametophyte is dominant.

sections.introduction:
When you look at a patch of moss, you are seeing only half of its life cycle. The green, leafy plant is the gametophyte. It is haploid—each cell has one set of chromosomes. It is the plant you see. It makes the gametes (sperm and eggs). But there is another generation—the sporophyte. It is attached to the gametophyte and looks like a tiny stalk with a capsule on top. It is diploid—cells have two sets of chromosomes. It makes spores.

This two-generation life cycle is called alternation of generations. It is found in all land plants, but the dominant generation changes. In bryophytes (mosses, liverworts, hornworts), the gametophyte is dominant. In ferns and flowering plants, the sporophyte is dominant.

Why does this matter? Because the bryophyte life cycle shows how plants adapted to life on land. Algae (the ancestors of plants) live in water. Their sperm swim to eggs. Land plants need to protect their eggs from drying out. Bryophytes evolved multicellular structures to protect eggs and sperm (archegonia and antheridia). They also evolved a multicellular sporophyte that makes many spores from one fertilization event. This was a big step in plant evolution.

But bryophytes still need water for fertilization. Their sperm have flagella and must swim. That is why mosses grow in damp places—under trees, on wet rocks, near streams. They cannot reproduce in dry conditions.

Understanding the bryophyte life cycle helps us understand how plants evolved, why mosses grow where they do, and how they reproduce.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing the alternation of generations: gametophyte (n) and sporophyte (2n) with arrows for fertilization and meiosis
introduction_image.caption: Alternation of generations: Gametophyte (n) → gametes (n) → fertilization (water) → zygote (2n) → sporophyte (2n) → meiosis → spores (n) → new gametophyte. Bryophytes have a dominant gametophyte.

key_concept.definition: Bryophytes have a gametophyte-dominant alternation of generations. The haploid (n) gametophyte is the familiar green plant; it produces gametes. The diploid (2n) sporophyte is attached to the gametophyte and produces spores via meiosis.
key_concept.context: The gametophyte is photosynthetic and independent. It produces antheridia (sperm) and archegonia (eggs). Fertilization requires water for sperm to swim. The zygote develops into the sporophyte, which consists of a foot (anchored in gametophyte), a seta (stalk), and a capsule (spore-producing). Meiosis in the capsule produces haploid spores. Spores are dispersed by wind and germinate into new gametophytes. This life cycle is ancestral for land plants and shows the transition from water-dependent reproduction to more complex terrestrial strategies.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of the moss life cycle with all stages: spore, protonema, gametophyte, antheridia, archegonia, fertilization, sporophyte, capsule, meiosis
image.caption: Moss life cycle: Spore (n) → protonema → gametophyte (n) → antheridia (sperm) and archegonia (eggs) → fertilization (water) → zygote (2n) → sporophyte (foot, seta, capsule) → meiosis → spores (n). Gametophyte is dominant and photosynthetic.

sections.key_concept: The bryophyte life cycle alternates between a dominant, photosynthetic gametophyte (n) that produces gametes, and a dependent sporophyte (2n) that produces spores via meiosis. Water is required for fertilization.

classifications.type: The Gametophyte: The Green Plant We See
classifications.value: Understanding the gametophyte explains the familiar moss plant and how it produces sperm and eggs.
characteristics: Haploid (n): Each cell has one set of chromosomes.
Photosynthetic: Green, makes its own food.
Dominant generation: Lives for a long time; the plant we see.
Produces gametes: In antheridia (sperm) and archegonia (eggs).
May reproduce asexually: By gemmae (in liverworts) or fragmentation.
classifications.behavior:
The moss gametophyte is the familiar green plant. It grows from a spore. The spore germinates into a filamentous protonema (a thread-like structure). The protonema grows and buds, producing the leafy shoots (gametophores) that we recognize as moss.

The gametophyte has rhizoids (root-like filaments) that anchor it, but it does not have true roots. It absorbs water and minerals directly through its leaves. It is small, which helps it survive in damp, shady places.

On the tips of the gametophyte, structures called antheridia and archegonia form. Antheridia are club-shaped and produce many sperm. The sperm have flagella (tails) and can swim through water. Archegonia are flask-shaped. Each contains a single egg. The neck of the archegonium opens when the egg is ready, releasing chemicals that attract sperm.

Some bryophytes (like liverworts) can also reproduce asexually. They produce gemmae—small, multicellular discs that are splashed out by raindrops and grow into new gametophytes. This allows them to spread without needing water for fertilization.

examples.process: Moss Gametophyte: From Spore to Leafy Shoots
examples.explanation:
A moss spore is tiny—smaller than a grain of sand. When it lands in a damp place, it germinates. The first thing it grows is a protonema—a green, thread-like structure. The protonema looks like algae. It grows by cell division at the tip.

After a while, buds form on the protonema. Each bud grows into a leafy shoot (gametophore). The gametophore has a stem and leaves. The leaves are only one cell thick in many mosses. They absorb water and carbon dioxide directly.

The gametophore grows by cell division at its tip. It can grow for months or years. It does not have true roots; it has rhizoids that anchor it to the substrate.

When the gametophore is mature, it produces gametangia at its tip. Antheridia (male) and archegonia (female) may be on the same plant or on different plants, depending on the species. Some mosses have separate male and female plants; others have both on the same plant.

image.alt: A diagram of moss development: spore → protonema → bud → leafy gametophore
image.caption: Moss gametophyte development: Spore (n) → protonema (filamentous) → bud → gametophore (leafy shoots). Gametophore is the familiar moss plant. Produces gametangia at tips.

examples.explanation:
Liverworts are a group of bryophytes. Some have flattened, ribbon-like bodies called thalli. Marchantia is a common liverwort with a thallus. The thallus grows flat on the soil. It has rhizoids on the underside for anchoring.

On the upper surface of the thallus are gemmae cups. Gemmae cups are small, cup-like structures. Inside are gemmae—tiny, disc-shaped pieces of tissue. When it rains, raindrops hit the cups and splash the gemmae out. The gemmae land nearby and grow into new thalli. This is asexual reproduction—no water needed for fertilization.

Marchantia also reproduces sexually. It produces specialized structures called gametophores. Male gametophores look like little umbrellas; they have antheridia on the top. Female gametophores look like little palm trees; they have archegonia under the rays. When it rains, water splashes sperm from the male gametophores onto the female gametophores. The sperm swim down the necks of the archegonia to fertilize the eggs.

This dual strategy—asexual (gemmae) and sexual (gametophores)—allows Marchantia to spread quickly in good conditions and also to produce genetic diversity.

image.alt: Marchantia thallus with gemmae cups and male/female gametophores
image.caption: Liverwort reproduction: Gemmae cups (asexual) produce gemmae that grow into new thalli. Male and female gametophores (sexual) produce sperm and eggs. Water splashes sperm to eggs.

classifications.type: The Sporophyte: The Stalk and Capsule
classifications.value: Understanding the sporophyte explains how mosses make and disperse spores.
characteristics: Diploid (2n): Each cell has two sets of chromosomes.
Dependent: Attached to and nourished by the gametophyte.
Ephemeral: Lives for a short time (weeks to months).
Structures: Foot (embedded in gametophyte), seta (stalk), capsule (spore-producing).
Produces spores: Via meiosis in the capsule.
classifications.behavior:
After fertilization, the zygote divides and grows into the sporophyte. The sporophyte is attached to the gametophyte by a structure called the foot. The foot is embedded in the gametophyte tissue and absorbs water and nutrients. The sporophyte cannot photosynthesize much (or at all in some species); it depends entirely on the gametophyte for food.

The seta is the stalk. In mosses, the seta elongates, pushing the capsule up above the gametophyte. This helps with spore dispersal. In liverworts, the seta is short; the capsule is not elevated much. In hornworts, the seta grows from a basal meristem and continues growing for a long time, like a horn.

The capsule is the spore-producing structure. Inside the capsule, cells called sporocytes undergo meiosis. Each sporocyte (2n) produces four haploid spores (n). The capsule may have special structures to help release the spores. In mosses, the capsule has a lid (operculum) and teeth (peristome). The teeth open when it is dry, allowing spores to be released, and close when it is wet, preventing release. This ensures that spores are released when the weather is dry and windy, so they can travel far. In liverworts, the capsule has elaters—cells that twist and help fling spores out. In hornworts, the capsule splits open lengthwise to release spores.

examples.explanation:
Look at a moss plant. You will see green, leafy shoots. Growing from some of them are reddish-brown stalks. Each stalk has a capsule at the top. This is the sporophyte.

The sporophyte grows from the archegonium after fertilization. The foot is embedded in the gametophyte. The seta elongates, pushing the capsule upward. The capsule is covered by a cap called the calyptra, which protects it as it develops. The calyptra falls off when the capsule is mature.

Under the calyptra, the capsule has a lid (operculum) and teeth (peristome). When the weather is dry, the peristome teeth bend outward, opening the capsule. Spores fall out and are carried by the wind. When the weather is wet, the teeth bend inward, closing the capsule. This prevents spores from being released when they would be heavy with moisture and would not travel far.

Inside the capsule, sporocytes (2n) undergo meiosis to produce haploid spores. One capsule can contain thousands of spores.

image.alt: Moss sporophyte with capsule, peristome teeth open (dry) and closed (wet)
image.caption: Moss sporophyte: Foot (in gametophyte), seta (stalk), capsule. Peristome teeth open when dry (spores released), close when wet (spores protected). Spores made by meiosis in capsule.

examples.explanation:
Hornworts are a group of bryophytes that look different from mosses and liverworts. Their sporophytes are long and horn-shaped—hence the name.

The hornwort sporophyte grows from a basal meristem. This means it continues growing at the base while the tip matures and releases spores. The sporophyte can grow for a long time, getting longer and longer. It is green and does some photosynthesis, but it still depends on the gametophyte for water and nutrients.

When the sporophyte is mature, it splits open lengthwise to release spores. Inside, there are pseudo-elaters—sterile cells that twist when dry, helping to fling the spores out.

Hornwort sporophytes are unique among bryophytes. They have stomata (pores for gas exchange) on their surface, like vascular plants. They also have chloroplasts and can photosynthesize. This is an intermediate step between the dependent sporophytes of mosses and the independent sporophytes of ferns and flowering plants.

image.alt: Hornwort sporophyte growing from thallus, with basal meristem and longitudinal splitting
image.caption: Hornwort sporophyte: Grows from basal meristem (continues growing). Splits open lengthwise to release spores. Has stomata and chloroplasts—more like vascular plants.

classifications.value: Understanding fertilization explains why bryophytes need water to reproduce and how they live in damp places.
characteristics: Flagellated sperm: Sperm have tails and swim through water.
Water requirement: Fertilization only possible when there is a film of water.
Chemotaxis: Sperm are attracted to chemicals released by archegonia.
Constraint: Limits bryophytes to moist habitats or wet periods.
Adaptations: Splash cups, dwarf males, asexual reproduction to reduce water dependency.
classifications.behavior:
Bryophyte sperm are like tiny tadpoles. They have flagella (tails) that beat and propel them through water. They need a film of water to swim in—rain, dew, or even water from melting snow. Without water, they die.

The archegonia release chemicals that attract sperm. The sperm swim toward the source of these chemicals. When they reach the archegonium, they swim down the neck to the egg. Only one sperm fertilizes the egg.

This water dependency explains why bryophytes are most common in damp places—under trees, on wet rocks, near streams. They can survive dry periods by going dormant, but they need water to reproduce.

Some bryophytes have adaptations to reduce water dependency. In Marchantia, the male gametophores are umbrella-shaped. When raindrops hit them, they splash sperm onto the female gametophores. This helps the sperm get closer to the eggs. In some mosses, dwarf males grow on the leaves of female plants. The sperm have a very short distance to swim. These adaptations help bryophytes reproduce even in places where water is not always abundant.

Bryophytes also reproduce asexually through gemmae and fragmentation. This allows them to spread without needing water for fertilization.

examples.explanation:
When the archegonium is mature, the neck canal cells break down. This creates a passage from the egg to the outside. The archegonium releases chemicals—including sugars and proteins—into the water. These chemicals attract sperm.

The sperm have receptors on their flagella that detect these chemicals. They swim toward higher concentrations. This is called chemotaxis. It ensures that sperm swim toward the archegonia, not away from them.

When a sperm reaches the archegonium, it swims down the neck. It passes through the neck canal and reaches the egg. The sperm and egg fuse, forming a diploid zygote.

This process is similar to what happens in ferns and some algae. It is an ancient way of reproducing that bryophytes have retained.

image.alt: Sperm swimming toward archegonium attracted by chemicals, with diagram of chemotaxis
image.caption: Fertilization: Archegonium releases chemicals → sperm detect chemicals → swim toward archegonium → swim down neck → fertilize egg. Water needed for swimming.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of the Bryophyte Life Cycle

principles.principle: Gametophyte Dominance Is an Ancient Strategy
principles.statement: In bryophytes, the haploid gametophyte is the dominant, long-lived generation. This is an ancient strategy that allowed early land plants to colonize terrestrial habitats.
principles.application:
The gametophyte is photosynthetic and independent. It produces the structures that make gametes. Because it is haploid, any mutation in its genes is immediately expressed. This allows natural selection to act quickly. If a mutation is harmful, the gametophyte dies. If it is beneficial, the gametophyte thrives and passes it on.

This is different from flowering plants, where the sporophyte is dominant and haploid stages are tiny and hidden. In flowering plants, harmful mutations can be hidden in the diploid stage.

Gametophyte dominance may have helped early land plants adapt to new environments. They could evolve quickly to survive drying, UV radiation, and other challenges.

In bryophytes, the sporophyte is dependent on the gametophyte. This ensures that spores are only produced when the gametophyte is healthy and has enough resources. If the gametophyte is stressed, it will not produce a sporophyte.
principles.example: Mosses can live in harsh environments—on rocks, in the Arctic, on tree bark. Their gametophytes are tough. They can dry out and survive. When it rains, they green up and start photosynthesizing again. This is called poikilohydry. Their ability to survive drying is an adaptation that allowed them to colonize land. The gametophyte's dominance means that the plant can survive as a haploid, even when conditions are poor. It does not need to invest in a sporophyte until conditions improve.
principles.analogy: The gametophyte is like a pioneer. It is small, tough, and can survive in harsh conditions. When conditions are good, it invests in making a sporophyte—like a parent investing in children. The sporophyte is the next generation, but it depends on the parent. This strategy works in environments where conditions are unpredictable.

image.alt: A diagram comparing bryophyte (gametophyte dominant) and flowering plant (sporophyte dominant) life cycles
image.caption: Gametophyte dominance: Bryophytes have a large, long-lived gametophyte (n). Flowering plants have a tiny, dependent gametophyte. Gametophyte dominance is an ancient strategy for colonizing land.

principles.principle: Water Dependency Is a Constraint and an Adaptation
principles.statement: Bryophytes need water for fertilization. This limits them to moist habitats, but it also allows them to reproduce when conditions are wet and spores can germinate.
principles.application:
The requirement for water means that bryophytes cannot reproduce in dry conditions. They are most abundant in places that are consistently damp—rainforests, stream banks, shaded forests. In deserts, they are rare and only grow in sheltered spots.

But water dependency also has advantages. When it rains, bryophytes can reproduce quickly. The water needed for fertilization also helps the spores germinate. The timing of reproduction is linked to conditions that are good for the next generation.

Some bryophytes have evolved ways to reduce water dependency. In Marchantia, raindrops splash sperm from male to female gametophores. In some mosses, dwarf males grow on the leaves of females, so the sperm have a short distance to swim. Many bryophytes also reproduce asexually through gemmae or fragmentation, which does not require water.

This mix of strategies—sexual reproduction when water is available, asexual reproduction when it is not—allows bryophytes to thrive in a range of environments.
principles.example: In a temperate forest, mosses reproduce in the spring. The snow melts, and there is plenty of water. The mosses grow and produce gametangia. Sperm swim through the water film on the moss. Fertilization occurs, and sporophytes develop. By summer, the capsules are mature. The peristome teeth open when it is dry, releasing spores into the wind. The spores land and wait for the next wet season to germinate. This cycle is timed to the seasons.
principles.analogy: Water dependency is like needing a boat to cross a river. If the river is there (rain), you can cross. If not, you wait. You can also build a bridge (asexual reproduction) to cross without a boat. Bryophytes use both strategies.

image.alt: A diagram showing bryophytes in a wet habitat (reproducing) and a dry habitat (dormant or using asexual reproduction)
image.caption: Water dependency: Bryophytes need water for fertilization. They reproduce sexually in wet conditions. In dry conditions, they may go dormant or reproduce asexually (gemmae, fragmentation).

principles.principle: Spore Dispersal Is Optimized for Wind
principles.statement: Bryophyte spores are tiny and lightweight. They are released when conditions are dry and windy, allowing them to travel long distances.
principles.application:
Spores are the dispersal units of bryophytes. They are haploid and very small—often less than 20 micrometers in diameter. They are produced in huge numbers. A single moss capsule can contain thousands of spores.

The capsule has adaptations to release spores when conditions are best. In mosses, the peristome teeth open when it is dry and close when it is wet. This prevents spores from being released in the rain, when they would be heavy and would not travel far. Instead, they are released in dry, windy weather, when they can be carried long distances.

In liverworts, elaters (twisting cells) help fling spores out. In hornworts, the capsule splits open lengthwise, and pseudo-elaters twist to release spores.

Spores are tough. They have thick walls made of sporopollenin, a resistant material. They can survive drying, UV radiation, and cold. They can remain viable for years, waiting for the right conditions to germinate.
principles.example: When a moss capsule is mature, the peristome teeth open on a dry day. Wind blows the spores out of the capsule. A single capsule can release millions of spores. They travel through the air and land on soil, rocks, or tree bark. Most will die, but a few will land in a damp place and germinate. This is how mosses colonize new areas.
principles.analogy: Spores are like dandelion seeds. They are light, they float in the air, and they travel far. The capsule is like a seed pod that opens when conditions are right. The plant invests in making many spores, hoping that a few will find a good place to grow.

image.alt: A diagram of moss capsule releasing spores, with peristome teeth open and wind carrying spores
image.caption: Spore dispersal: Peristome teeth open when dry → wind carries spores away. Spores are tiny, lightweight, and tough. They can travel long distances and survive for years.

sections.section_title: Key Components of the Bryophyte Life Cycle

components.definition: The bryophyte life cycle has three key components: the gametophyte (haploid, dominant, photosynthetic), the sporophyte (diploid, dependent, spore-producing), and the processes that connect them (fertilization, meiosis).
components.interpretation: For a bryophyte to complete its life cycle, a spore must germinate, the gametophyte must grow and produce gametes, water must be present for fertilization, the sporophyte must develop, and meiosis must produce new spores. If any step fails, the cycle stops. This is why bryophytes are common in damp places—they need water for fertilization.

image.alt: A diagram of the bryophyte life cycle with the three main components labeled: gametophyte, sporophyte, and spores
image.caption: Life cycle components: Gametophyte (n, dominant) → fertilization (water) → sporophyte (2n, dependent) → meiosis → spores (n) → gametophyte. All components must work for reproduction.

categories.description: Antheridia and archegonia are multicellular structures that protect the gametes from drying out. This was a key adaptation for life on land.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Antheridia are male gametangia. They are club-shaped or spherical. Inside, many sperm are produced by mitosis. The sperm have flagella and can swim. When the antheridium is mature, it opens, releasing the sperm into a film of water.

Archegonia are female gametangia. They are flask-shaped, with a neck and a swollen base (venter). Inside the venter is a single egg. The neck contains neck canal cells. When the archegonium is mature, the neck canal cells break down, creating a passage for sperm. The archegonium also releases chemicals that attract sperm.

These structures protect the gametes from drying out. In algae, gametes are released directly into the water. In bryophytes, they are protected within the gametophyte. This allowed plants to reproduce on land, even though they still needed water for the sperm to swim.

examples.explanation:
The archegonium is a flask-shaped structure. It has a long neck and a swollen base (venter). The venter contains the egg and a ventral canal cell. The neck contains neck canal cells.

When the egg is mature, the neck canal cells and ventral canal cell break down. This creates a channel from the outside to the egg. The archegonium also secretes chemicals that attract sperm.

Sperm swim down the neck, following the chemical gradient. When a sperm reaches the egg, it fertilizes it. The zygote remains inside the archegonium, protected from drying. It begins to divide and grow into the sporophyte.

The archegonium is a key adaptation for land plants. It protects the egg and the young sporophyte from desiccation. Similar structures are found in ferns and some other plants.

image.alt: A diagram of an archegonium showing neck, venter, egg, and sperm entering
image.caption: Archegonium: Flask-shaped structure with neck and venter. Egg in venter. Neck canal cells break down to create passage. Chemicals attract sperm. Protects egg and young sporophyte.

categories.description: Bryophytes have evolved various ways to release their spores, from peristome teeth to elaters to splitting capsules.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Mosses: The capsule has a lid (operculum) and peristome teeth. The teeth are hygroscopic—they move with changes in humidity. When dry, they bend outward, opening the capsule. When wet, they bend inward, closing it. This ensures that spores are released in dry, windy weather.

Liverworts: The capsule has elaters—cells with spiral thickenings. When the capsule dries, the elaters twist and untwist, helping to fling the spores out. The capsule may also split open along lines.

Hornworts: The capsule splits open lengthwise. Pseudo-elaters (sterile cells) twist when dry, helping to release the spores. The sporophyte continues growing from the base while the tip releases spores, allowing prolonged spore release.

examples.explanation:
The peristome teeth of moss capsules are made of cell walls that are sensitive to humidity. They are arranged around the opening of the capsule.

When the air is dry, the teeth bend outward. This opens the capsule, and spores can fall out. Wind carries them away.

When the air is wet, the teeth bend inward, closing the capsule. This prevents spores from being released when they would be heavy with moisture and would not travel far.

This mechanism is simple but effective. It ensures that spores are released at the best time for dispersal. Some mosses have two rings of teeth (double peristome) that move independently, giving even more control.

image.alt: Moss capsule with peristome teeth open (dry) and closed (wet)
image.caption: Peristome teeth: Open when dry (spores released), closed when wet (spores protected). Ensures spores are dispersed in dry, windy conditions for maximum distance.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Tracing the Moss Life Cycle

worked_examples.title: Determining Ploidy and Processes
worked_examples.problem: You are studying the life cycle of a moss (Polytrichum). Identify the ploidy (n or 2n) and the process (mitosis, meiosis, or fertilization) for each stage: (1) Spore; (2) Protonema; (3) Gametophyte leaf; (4) Antheridium; (5) Sperm; (6) Archegonium; (7) Egg; (8) Zygote; (9) Sporophyte capsule; (10) Spore mother cell (sporocyte). Then answer: (11) If a gametophyte cell has 14 chromosomes, how many chromosomes are in a sporophyte cell? (12) How many in a spore?

step_by_step.explanation: Determine ploidy and process for gametophyte stages.
step_by_step.calculation: Spore: haploid (n), product of meiosis. Protonema: haploid (n), grows from spore by mitosis. Gametophyte leaf: haploid (n), grows by mitosis. Antheridium: haploid (n), develops from gametophyte by mitosis. Sperm: haploid (n), produced in antheridium by mitosis. Archegonium: haploid (n), develops from gametophyte by mitosis. Egg: haploid (n), produced in archegonium by mitosis. All gametophyte structures are haploid and grow by mitosis.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine ploidy and process for sporophyte stages.
step_by_step.calculation: Zygote: diploid (2n), formed by fertilization (sperm + egg). Sporophyte capsule: diploid (2n), develops from zygote by mitosis. Spore mother cell (sporocyte): diploid (2n), in capsule, undergoes meiosis. Spore: haploid (n), product of meiosis.
Gametophyte cell = 14 chromosomes (n = 14). Sporophyte cell (2n) = 28 chromosomes. Spore (n) = 14 chromosomes. Meiosis reduces 2n to n; fertilization restores 2n.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Spore: n, meiosis; (2) Protonema: n, mitosis; (3) Gametophyte leaf: n, mitosis; (4) Antheridium: n, mitosis; (5) Sperm: n, mitosis; (6) Archegonium: n, mitosis; (7) Egg: n, mitosis; (8) Zygote: 2n, fertilization; (9) Sporophyte capsule: 2n, mitosis; (10) Sporocyte: 2n, meiosis; (11) Sporophyte cell: 28 chromosomes; (12) Spore: 14 chromosomes. This shows the alternation: haploid gametophyte (mitosis) → fertilization → diploid sporophyte (meiosis) → haploid spores.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bryophyte life cycle alternates between haploid (n) and diploid (2n) generations. Gametophyte (n) produces gametes by mitosis. Fertilization produces zygote (2n). Sporophyte (2n) produces spores by meiosis. Chromosome number is halved in meiosis and restored in fertilization.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: The Moss Life Cycle
sections.introduction: The moss life cycle follows a sequence from spore to gametophyte to sporophyte and back to spore. Understanding this flow reveals how mosses reproduce and disperse.

process_flow.description: A haploid spore lands in a damp place, absorbs water, and germinates into a protonema.
process_flow.mechanism: Spore imbibes water → metabolic activation → germ tube emerges → grows into filamentous protonema (chloronema → caulonema). Protonema is green and photosynthetic. Buds form on protonema.
process_flow.result: Protonema established; buds ready to grow into gametophores.

image.alt: Moss spore germinating into protonema, then buds forming
image.caption: Step 1: Spore (n) → protonema (filamentous) → buds. Protonema grows quickly to establish the plant.

process_flow.description: Buds grow into leafy gametophores. Antheridia and archegonia form at the tips.
process_flow.mechanism: Buds grow via apical cell division → leafy gametophore develops. At maturity, antheridia (sperm) and/or archegonia (eggs) form at tips. Some species have separate male and female plants; others have both on same plant.
process_flow.result: Mature gametophyte; gametes ready for fertilization.

image.alt: Moss gametophore with antheridia and archegonia at tips
image.caption: Step 2: Gametophore grows from bud. Antheridia (sperm) and archegonia (eggs) form at tips. Water needed for sperm to swim to eggs.

process_flow.description: Sperm swim through water to archegonia, fertilize eggs. Zygote grows into sporophyte.
process_flow.mechanism: Water film enables sperm to swim to archegonia → fertilization → zygote (2n) → zygote divides → sporophyte develops (foot, seta, capsule). Sporophyte remains attached to gametophyte, receiving nutrients.
process_flow.result: Sporophyte produces spores via meiosis in capsule.

image.alt: Sperm swimming to archegonium, zygote developing into sporophyte with capsule
image.caption: Step 3: Fertilization (water) → zygote (2n) → sporophyte (foot, seta, capsule). Capsule produces spores via meiosis. Spores released and dispersed.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Evolution of the Bryophyte Life Cycle
sections.definition: The bryophyte life cycle is an intermediate between algae and vascular plants. It shows how plants evolved to live on land while retaining some ancestral traits.

process_types.energy_profile: Bryophytes evolved from green algae. They kept water-dependent fertilization but developed multicellular gametangia and a multicellular sporophyte.
process_types.direction: Algae (haploid dominant, unicellular zygote) → Bryophytes (haploid dominant, multicellular sporophyte) → Vascular plants (diploid dominant)
process_types.function: Protect gametes from drying out, produce many spores from one fertilization, disperse spores by wind
goals: Increase reproductive output (many spores from one fertilization)
Disperse offspring by wind (spores instead of swimming zygotes)
Retain water-dependent fertilization as an ancestral trait
process_types.energy_source: Gametophyte photosynthesis supports both generations; sporophyte is dependent.

primary_example.process: Sporophyte Evolution: From Single Cell to Multicellular Structure
primary_example.explanation:
In algae, the zygote is the only diploid stage. It undergoes meiosis immediately, producing four haploid cells. This is called zygotic meiosis.

In bryophytes, the zygote divides by mitosis first, forming a multicellular sporophyte. This sporophyte then undergoes meiosis, producing many spores. This is called sporic meiosis.

This was a major innovation. Instead of one fertilization producing one offspring (the zygote), one fertilization can produce thousands of spores. This greatly increases reproductive output.

The sporophyte is still dependent on the gametophyte. It does not have its own roots; it gets water and nutrients from the gametophyte. This is an intermediate stage. In ferns and flowering plants, the sporophyte becomes independent and dominant.

image.alt: Comparison of algal zygotic meiosis and bryophyte sporic meiosis
image.caption: Sporophyte evolution: Algae (zygotic meiosis: zygote → meiosis → spores). Bryophytes (sporic meiosis: zygote → mitosis → multicellular sporophyte → meiosis → many spores). More spores from one fertilization.

image.alt: Evolutionary tree showing algae → bryophytes → ferns → seed plants
image.caption: Plant evolution: Algae (haploid dominant) → Bryophytes (haploid dominant, multicellular sporophyte) → Ferns (sporophyte dominant, independent) → Seed plants (sporophyte dominant, seeds). Bryophytes are the first land plants.

process_types.energy_profile: Asexual reproduction (gemmae, fragmentation) uses less energy and does not require water. Sexual reproduction (spores) uses more energy but generates diversity.
process_types.direction: Favorable conditions: asexual reproduction (rapid spread). Stressful conditions: sexual reproduction (genetic diversity, spores that can disperse).
process_types.function: Balance rapid colonization with genetic diversity
goals: Produce resistant spores for long-distance dispersal (sexual)
process_types.energy_source: Asexual: vegetative growth; Sexual: gametangia production, sporophyte development.

primary_example.explanation:
Marchantia uses both strategies. Gemmae cups produce gemmae that are splashed out by raindrops. Each gemma grows into a new thallus. This is fast and does not need water for fertilization. It is good for colonizing nearby areas.

When conditions are good, Marchantia also produces male and female gametophores. Sperm swim to eggs when it rains. The resulting sporophytes produce spores that can travel far on the wind. This generates genetic diversity and allows colonization of distant areas.

By using both strategies, Marchantia can spread quickly in good conditions and also produce diverse offspring that can adapt to new environments.

image.alt: Marchantia with gemmae cups (asexual) and gametophores (sexual)
image.caption: Dual strategy: Asexual (gemmae) for rapid local spread. Sexual (spores) for genetic diversity and long-distance dispersal. Both strategies together maximize success.

image.alt: A diagram showing when asexual vs. sexual reproduction is favored in bryophytes
image.caption: Reproductive balance: Asexual reproduction (gemmae, fragmentation) favored in favorable conditions for rapid spread. Sexual reproduction (spores) favored for genetic diversity and long-distance dispersal. Bryophytes use both.

sections.relationship: Evolutionary transitions and reproductive strategies are connected. The evolution of a multicellular sporophyte allowed bryophytes to produce many spores from one fertilization. The retention of water-dependent fertilization kept them tied to moist habitats. Asexual reproduction allowed them to spread even when water was scarce.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding the bryophyte life cycle. Knowing these terms will help you describe how mosses and their relatives reproduce.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in the bryophyte life cycle. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss alternation of generations, gametophytes, sporophytes, and reproduction in bryophytes.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of a moss with key terms: gametophyte, sporophyte, capsule, seta, foot, antheridia, archegonia, rhizoids
image.caption: Key bryophyte terms: Knowing these structures helps you understand the life cycle.

key_properties.explanation: The dominant generation in bryophytes. It is haploid (n) and photosynthetic. Produces gametes (sperm and eggs). The familiar green moss plant is the gametophyte.
The dependent generation in bryophytes. It is diploid (2n) and attached to the gametophyte. Produces spores via meiosis. Consists of foot, seta, and capsule.
A life cycle that alternates between a haploid (n) gametophyte and a diploid (2n) sporophyte. Found in all land plants and some algae.
Male gametangium. Produces flagellated sperm by mitosis. Club-shaped or spherical.
Female gametangium. Flask-shaped, with a neck and a venter. Produces one egg. The neck canal breaks down to allow sperm entry.
The filamentous stage that grows from a moss spore. Later produces buds that grow into leafy gametophores.
The leafy shoot of a moss gametophyte. Bears the gametangia.
The base of the sporophyte, embedded in the gametophyte. Absorbs water and nutrients from the gametophyte.
The stalk of the sporophyte. Elongates to elevate the capsule for spore dispersal.
The spore-producing part of the sporophyte. Contains sporocytes that undergo meiosis to produce haploid spores.
Teeth around the opening of a moss capsule. Open when dry (spores released), close when wet (spores protected). Hygroscopic.
Cells in liverwort capsules that twist when dry, helping to fling spores out.
Small, multicellular discs produced in gemmae cups on liverwort thalli. Splashed out by raindrops and grow into new gametophytes. Asexual reproduction.
A flattened, ribbon-like body. Found in some liverworts (Marchantia) and hornworts.
Root-like filaments that anchor the gametophyte. Not true roots; they absorb water but do not have vascular tissue.
Having one set of chromosomes (n). Gametophytes are haploid; spores are haploid.
Having two sets of chromosomes (2n). Sporophytes are diploid; zygotes are diploid.
Cell division that reduces chromosome number from diploid to haploid. Produces spores in the sporophyte capsule.
The fusion of sperm and egg to form a diploid zygote. Requires water for sperm to swim.
A tough, resistant material in spore walls. Protects spores from drying, UV radiation, and decay.

summary:
Bryophytes (mosses, liverworts, hornworts) have a life cycle with alternating generations. The haploid (n) gametophyte is the dominant, photosynthetic, long-lived generation. It produces gametes in antheridia (sperm) and archegonia (eggs). Fertilization requires water for the flagellated sperm to swim to the eggs. The diploid (2n) zygote develops into a sporophyte that remains attached to and dependent on the gametophyte. The sporophyte consists of a foot (anchored in gametophyte), a seta (stalk), and a capsule (spore-producing). Meiosis in the capsule produces haploid spores. Spores are dispersed by wind and germinate into new gametophytes, completing the cycle.

This life cycle is ancestral for land plants. It shows the transition from algae (haploid dominant, water-dependent) to vascular plants (sporophyte dominant, independent). Bryophytes have retained water-dependent fertilization but have evolved multicellular gametangia to protect gametes and a multicellular sporophyte to produce many spores from one fertilization.

Variations exist among bryophytes. Mosses have leafy gametophytes and sporophytes with peristome teeth for spore release. Liverworts may have thalloid or leafy gametophytes; some produce gemmae for asexual reproduction. Hornworts have sporophytes that grow from a basal meristem and have stomata.

Understanding the bryophyte life cycle helps us understand plant evolution, why bryophytes live in damp places, and how they reproduce and disperse.
